title-group.article-title: Airborne Algae: A Rising Public Health Risk

contrib-group.aff: Environmental Molecular Sciences Laboratory, Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, Richland, Washington 99354, United States

permissions.copyright-statement: Not subject to U.S. Copyright. Published 2023 by American Chemical Society

license.license-p: Permits non-commercial access and re-use, provided that author attribution and integrity are maintained; but does not permit creation of adaptations or other derivative works (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

body.p: Harmful algal blooms (HABs) are known to produce toxins that threaten human and animal health. Absorption of HAB toxins through the skin and ingestion can cause health risks such as dermatitis and respiratory allergies,1 but the inhalation of these toxins remains an understudied threat. Considering the recent global pandemic driven by aerosol-based transmission of SARS-CoV-2, new developments in science and policy should prioritize the health impacts of air quality and composition. These air quality considerations must expand past viruses and other well-documented primary biological atmospheric particles to include the rising public health risk from airborne HAB toxins and consider policies focused on research, education, and regulatory action. Currently, airborne HAB species and toxins are the least-studied organisms in both aerobiological and phycological (the scientific study of algae) studies.2

HABs occur when aquatic photosynthetic organisms, primarily cyanobacteria, dinoflagellates, and diatoms, grow rampant, use up the oxygen in the water, and produce toxins.1 HABs can be caused by increasing water temperatures, stagnant water, and a high concentration of nutrients (phosphorus and nitrogen) caused by fertilizer runoffs.3 People can be exposed to HAB toxins through skin contact, ingesting contaminated water, consuming food with HAB toxins, and inhaling airborne droplets that contain toxins.3 Importantly, reports have shown increased sensitivity to inhaled toxins compared to ingestion.2 These toxins can range from dermatoxins (like aplysiatoxin and lyngbyatoxin-a) that impact the skin, hepatotoxins (such as microcystin, anabaenopeptin, and nodularin) and cytotoxins (like cylindrospermopsins) that damage the liver and other internal organs, and neurotoxins that affect the central nervous system.3,4 Some HAB neurotoxins, such as β-N-methylamino-l-alanine (BMAA),5 are suggested to have a role in certain cases of Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).6 Given the potential severity of these toxins, states like Florida that are especially at risk for HABs regularly monitor their waters for both the toxins and the species that produce them.7 While HABs and their toxins are monitored in water, the levels of algae and toxins in the air, within aerosols, are not.

Aerosols, commonly associated with the fine mist of perfume or paint, are sprayed suspensions of particles released from bursting bubbles at the air–water interface.8 Their contents comprise the enriched organic and inorganic matter that was at the water surface.9 Because algae are quite abundant in coastal waters, they contribute to the enriched matter at the air–water interface. While most algal matter is not harmful, algal cells and fragments that include harmful toxins released during HABs can become aerosolized and the abundance of microalgae in the atmosphere can reach 10–4 to 104 cells/m3.1 Aerosols can also range in size from fine (<2.5 μm) to coarse (2.5–10 μm), with toxins mainly present in the fine aerosols and algal cells present in the coarse ones.8 Although the most recognized danger is from the aerosolization of HAB toxins, more research is needed to understand the health impacts of aerosolized algal cells as allergens. The only common level of mask available to protect against algal toxins and allergens is the N95 mask, which protects from a particle diameter of 0.1–2 μm and larger.10 Coastal communities in particular should be vigilant during windy days as aerosol generation begins at wind speeds greater than ∼11 mph.11

Aerosol production is increased during natural extreme weather events like monsoons and hurricanes where wind speeds can reach 150 mph.5,11 As winds break the water surface and release aerosols into the air, coastal communities and those surrounding (up to 6000 miles from HAB outbreaks) can be exposed for hours to weeks.1,11 The increased aerosol production during extreme weather events presents potentially higher concentrations of airborne algae with higher rates of HAB toxin deposition in human and animal respiratory tracts.1,5 The persistence of airborne algae from such events can even introduce HAB toxins into buildings and indoor environments, extending their public health threat.2 The greater generation of airborne algae, combined with the high wind speeds of weather events, presents an opportunity for biogeographic expansion of HAB species into new bodies of water, introducing competition between algal species and bioaccumulation of toxins in the food chain. Additionally, as climate change results in warming global water temperatures, the frequency and extremity of natural weather events will increase, causing greater aerosolization of algae and HAB toxins.

There has been limited discussion of how the compounding events of HABs and extreme weather make aerosolized algal toxins a more severe and frequent threat. The synergistic interplay of climate change and HAB outbreaks produces an uncertain abundance of airborne toxins that requires new consideration as an air quality threat, as shown in Figure 1. While there is policy dedicated to managing aquatic HAB toxin levels,12 no management guidelines for airborne algal toxins have been developed by U.S. federal agencies or the World Health Organization.13 Therefore, more research must be conducted to quantify the danger that aerosolized HABs pose, and the following public health policies should be considered: (1) develop an air quality metric for HAB toxins in aerosols, similar to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency drinking water advisory of 1.6 μg/L total microcystin,4 so that citizens can make informed choices about risk mitigation, (2) educate the populace, especially at-risk groups like the asthmatic and immunocompromised, about the use of masks to prevent the inhalation of harmful aerosolized particle matter during HAB season and extreme weather events, and (3) expand and extend restrictions on the use of fertilizers that cause algal blooms in areas prone to HABs and forecasted to be hit by extreme weather events as already regulated in certain counties in Florida.7

caption.p: Process schematic for the aerosolization of algae. Algal blooms concentrate at the ocean surface and become aerosolized via wave breaking. This process is intensified by extreme weather events that spread algae and their toxins to coastal and inland communities, posing a public health risk to vulnerable populations.

notes.p: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

bio.p: Dr. Mickey Rogers is a Linus Pauling Distinguished Postdoctoral Fellow within the Terrestrial-Atmospheric Processes team at Pacific Northwest National Laboratory. She is studying biological aerosol with a focus on airborne algal cells and fragments that become aerosolized in our atmosphere. Her research uses electron microscopy and mass spectrometry to explore innovative atmospheric and industrial applications for airborne algae, including new carbon sequestration pathways. Mickey is passionate about translating science into nontechnical language to engage with policymakers and future generations of researchers.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: M.M.R. acknowledges the Laboratory Directed Research and Development Program at Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, a multiprogram national laboratory operated by Battelle for the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE). M.M.R. is grateful for the support of the Linus Pauling Distinguished Postdoctoral Fellowship program and Proposal Scope 80996. R.K.S. acknowledges the Environmental Molecular Sciences Laboratory (EMSL), a DOE Office of Science User Facility sponsored by the Environmental and Biological Science Directorate. The authors thank S. China and A. Rogers for insightful comments on the manuscript. The entirety of this research was performed on Project 10.46936/staf.proj.2022.60647/60008762 and used resources at the EMSL, which is a DOE Office of Science User Facility. The EMSL is sponsored by the Biological and Environmental Research program and operated under Contract DE-AC05-76RL01830.
